metadata.title: Research the Hallmarks of Cancer: Are the 2022 Additions Real Hallmarks?

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Merhaba, Liam — in for Bear. Hallmarks 2022 — settled or guessed? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.segment: Research the Hallmarks of Cancer: Are

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Hallmarks 2022 — settled or guessed?

Hanahan added phenotypic plasticity, polymorphic microbiomes, and senescent cells to the cancer hallmarks in 2022. The chapter flags all three as contested. The question: are they real mechanistic additions or educated hedges? The framework keeps growing — but a list with no stopping rule is either a living theory or an unfalsifiable one.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — title card: 2022 Hallmarks additions listed; contested flag visible

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to research the 2022 additions and build a comparison table — strongest supporting evidence and strongest rebuttal for each.

props.command:
claude "Research the 2022 Hallmarks of Cancer additions:
phenotypic plasticity, polymorphic microbiomes,
senescent cells. For each, find the strongest
supporting evidence and the strongest rebuttal.
Produce a table: hallmark | key supporting studies
| key challenges | consensus status"

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) -- Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the code before trusting it. The script parses Claude's table and flags each hallmark's evidence tier — robust, preclinical only, or proposed. It checks whether any randomized clinical trial supports the addition or whether the evidence is entirely observational and mechanistic.

props.code:
# hallmarks_audit.py -- Claude Code output
HALLMARKS = [
  {"name": "Phenotypic plasticity",
   "support": "EMT studies, cancer stem cell lit",
   "rebuttal": "No RCT; plasticity is descriptive",
   "tier": "PROPOSED"},
  {"name": "Polymorphic microbiomes",
   "support": "Fusobacterium in CRC, FMT studies",
   "rebuttal": "Causal chain unproven; correlation only",
   "tier": "CONTESTED"},
  {"name": "Senescent cells",
   "support": "SASP in tumor microenvironment",
   "rebuttal": "Context-dependent; some anti-tumor roles",
   "tier": "PROPOSED"},
]
for h in HALLMARKS:
  print(f"{h['name']}: [{h['tier']}]")
  print(f"  Support: {h['support']}")
  print(f"  Rebuttal: {h['rebuttal']}")

beats.narration_text: Run it. The comparison table prints: phenotypic plasticity flagged PROPOSED — EMT studies support it but no RCT exists. Polymorphic microbiomes flagged CONTESTED — correlation with colorectal cancer, causal chain unproven. Senescent cells flagged PROPOSED — SASP promotes tumor microenvironment but the role is context-dependent.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording: comparison table with evidence tiers; newer additions show weaker evidence column

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to add the column that separates a framework from a checklist: what experiment would definitively settle each question?

props.command:
claude "Add a column: what experiment would
definitively settle whether each 2022 addition
is a true mechanistic hallmark vs. a descriptive
category?"

beats.narration_text: Revised table. Phenotypic plasticity: a lineage-tracing study showing irreversible state transitions drives the cancer — some already running. Polymorphic microbiomes: a germ-free mouse model with defined microbiome transfer and tumor induction — proposed. Senescent cell experiment: selective senescent cell ablation in established tumors and measure recurrence — early trials exist.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording: table with experiment column; some entries note trials already running

beats.narration_text: The framework keeps growing because it absorbs anomalies rather than resolving them. That is both its strength and its weakness. A list with no stopping rule is useful for organizing research and dangerous for making clinical decisions. The original six hallmarks had mechanistic experiments behind them. The 2022 additions have correlative observations and plausible mechanisms. That gap is the whole debate.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: original 6 hallmarks vs 2022 additions; evidence tier contrast

beats.narration_text: Read Hanahan 2022 in Cancer Discovery and score each new addition against the original criteria he used in 2000. The hedging language is in the text itself. Count the modal verbs — may, might, could — against declarative statements. That ratio tells you how confident the author actually is.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps card: read Hanahan 2022, score additions against original criteria

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. The framework keeps growing because it absorbs anomalies rather than resolving them. That is both its strength and its weakness. The 2022 additions have correlative observations and plausible mechanisms. That gap is the whole debate. beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research the Hallmarks of Cancer: Are the 2022 Additions Real Hallmark] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
